description: Stresses reporting-relationship extraction across phrasings: 'I report to X', 'X's manager', 'reports up to', 'X's boss'. Traps: skip-level misread (Barry does NOT report to Janet), hierarchical relationship downgraded to collaborates_with (the production coercion bug), and a former-employer manager anecdote (Accenture) that must produce nothing.

meeting.transcript:
**Stephen Cole**: Staffing review for the new engagement. Barry, you first — how's your capacity?

**Barry Chen**: I can take the workstream lead. Since I report to Stephen it keeps the escalation path simple — anything blocked goes straight up to him.

**Lauren Park**: Works for me. On the data side, Nadia Hassan is the right fit. She's on my data science team and she's already deep in the Atlas migration, so the context carries over.

**Stephen Cole**: Good. Nadia reports up to you, Lauren, so her utilization call is yours.

**Lauren Park**: I run the data science team, so yes — I'll rebalance her sprint load. She stays on Atlas through the staging milestone, then shifts.

**Barry Chen**: One question on governance. If the client asks who signs off above Stephen — that's Janet Wu now, right?

**Stephen Cole**: Right, Janet Wu is my boss since the reorg, so for you she's the skip-level. Sign-offs above me go to her.

**Barry Chen**: Got it. Funny — my old manager back at Accenture used to make us chart this stuff out every quarter. Old habits.

**Lauren Park**: Not the worst habit. So: Barry leads the workstream, Nadia covers data under me, Stephen escalates to Janet where needed.

**Stephen Cole**: That's the structure. I'll confirm Janet's availability for the kickoff sign-off this week.

**Barry Chen**: And I'll draft the RACI for the workstream by Monday.

**Stephen Cole**: Perfect. Short and sweet, thanks both.
